items.q: Was the Hijrah simply an escape from danger?
items.correct: No - it was the founding moment of the Islamic community: in Madinah the Muslims built their own institutions, and the Islamic calendar dates from the Hijrah, not from the Prophet's birth or first revelation.
items.misconception: The Hijrah was just a retreat with no lasting significance.
items.random_wrong: The Hijrah was a pilgrimage made every year by the early Muslims.
items.why: Students miss why year 1 of the calendar is 622: the move created the ummah as a functioning community.

items.q: Was the new community in Madinah organised around tribal loyalty?
items.correct: No - the ummah replaced tribal loyalty with loyalty based on faith, with the Muhajirun and Ansar paired as brothers across old tribal lines.
items.misconception: Early Islam kept the old tribal system unchanged.
items.random_wrong: Membership of the ummah depended on wealth.
items.why: The shift from tribe to faith-community is the central social change the topic examines.

items.q: Did only Muslims live in early Madinah?
items.correct: No - the Charter of Madinah included the Jewish and other tribes of the city, setting out mutual rights and duties, including joint defence.
items.misconception: Madinah under the Prophet was a city with no non-Muslim inhabitants.
items.random_wrong: Madinah was an empty desert before the Muslims arrived.
items.why: The Charter shows the first community managing religious diversity, a point students overlook when they imagine a uniform city.

items.q: What does the Hijrah refer to?
items.correct: The migration of the Muslims from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE
items.misconception: The first revelation of the Qur'an
items.why: Correct answer: The migration of the Muslims from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE. “The first revelation of the Qur'an” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The Muslims of Madinah who helped the emigrants from Makkah
items.misconception: The leaders of the Quraysh in Makkah
items.why: It's The Muslims of Madinah who helped the emigrants from Makkah. “The leaders of the Quraysh in Makkah” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which battle of 624 was the first major Muslim victory?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Badr. “Uhud” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What happened when the Muslims entered Makkah in 630?
items.correct: The city was taken largely peacefully, the idols were removed from the Ka'bah, and a general amnesty was given
items.misconception: The city was burned to the ground
items.random_wrong: The Muslims were driven out again
items.why: It's The city was taken largely peacefully, the idols were removed from the Ka'bah, and a general amnesty was given. “The city was burned to the ground” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year CE did the Hijrah take place?
items.why: Exact answer: 622 CE. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
